data.title: Power Integrations: Drivers Sending The Stock Vertical Do Not Look Sturdy Enough
data.description:
Power Integrations (POWI), a supplier of high-voltage power conversion solutions for the semiconductor industry, was essentially stuck with the stock basically going sideways for eight months or so. That changed in April when POWI went pretty much vertical by gaining as much as 48.9% at one point. However, the foundation supporting this large and rapid appreciation in the stock price does not appear to be very solid. It seems to rely rather extensively on assumptions, especially as it relates to future growth, something that could become an issue with the next earnings report due on May 7.

POWI comes back to life in a big way

In my September 2025 article about POWI, I mentioned how POWI’s decision to essentially make a fresh start with a new CEO was probably unavoidable with the way the company had underperformed for years. The company had, for instance, made a number of what eventually turned out to be overly optimistic growth projections into the future by prematurely calling for a recovery in demand, which it was forced to subsequently retract as it became clear demand would not be as strong. This change at the helm could conceivably result in POWI doing better, but I also pointed out how the stock remained in a downtrend, which went back several years. Accordingly, I decided to rate POWI a hold in the article.

The chart above shows how the prior rating proved to be on point for months with the stock basically going sideways, mostly in the forties, which is where the stock traded when the aforementioned article was written. Notice, for instance, how the stock stayed below the $50 price point, starting in August, for well over half a year, as shown in the chart.

This stayed true up to and including March, before POWI made its way into the fifties, which paved the way for a powerful rally in April that went pretty much vertical at one point. This resulted in POWI breaking through the long-term trendline mentioned in the previous article. The chart below shows how POWI accelerated once it managed to get past the descending trendline that had kept it contained for years.

It’s worth mentioning that POWI was not the only semi that went into overdrive in April. The whole sector rallied in April, which appeared to have rubbed off on POWI as it got a lift from semis going higher. Capital flowed into semiconductors, in large part due to AI, and POWI seems to have benefited from this dynamic as a semiconductor stock.

As long as the aforementioned trendline was in place, a strong argument against long POWI existed, but with the trend broken, this argument is no longer applicable. This very likely assisted with the recent acceleration in the stock price. On the other hand, the surge caused the stock to become very overbought, which likely played a role in causing the stock to pull back in the last few days. Some short covering may also have played a role.

Why the upcoming report could become a catalyst

Another reason that likely played a part is that valuations had become so elevated, which remains true even with the stock off the highs. For instance, POWI has a P/E ratio of 52.8x with TTM non-GAAP EPS of $1.25, and the stock last closed at $66.02, which is not only more than double the median in the sector but also well above the low thirties POWI averaged in the past five years.

Keep in mind that this is with the stock ceding a portion of the recent gains. POWI is still up 85.8% YTD after gaining as much as 114.4%, which gives one a sense of how elevated valuations got during the April spike. These elevated valuations likely convinced some to lower their exposure after the recent surge in the stock price, which contributed to the stock retreating in the past few days.

Another issue likely on people’s minds is the upcoming Q1 FY2026 report, which is scheduled to be released on May 7. My estimate is that POWI will announce Q1 FY2026 non-GAAP EPS of $0.24 on revenue of $106.5M, or somewhat better than the $0.23 POWI earned on revenue of $103.2M in the preceding Q4 FY2025 report.

Yet both would be worse than what POWI achieved a year before when it earned $0.31 in Q1 FY2025 and $0.30 in Q4 FY2024. In fact, it is worth mentioning that although the stock price has soared higher, quarterly results have not improved anywhere as much. The chart posted earlier shows how the stock price fell in 2023, and this had everything to do with the downturn in demand that caused sales and profits to contract.

For instance, GAAP EPS shrank in each of the past three completed fiscal years, or FY2023-2025, after going from $2.93 in FY2022 to just $0.39 in FY2025, as announced in the Form 10-K released in February. In terms of non-GAAP, non-GAAP EPS fell from $3.29 in FY2022 to $1.25 in FY2025, although this was better than the $1.16 of FY2024. FY2025 revenue grew by 6% YoY to $443.5M. The balance sheet is also fine with just $19M in debt, more than offset by cash, cash equivalents, and short-term marketable securities totaling $249.5M.

POWI has not done all that much recently to support a rally in the stock price as large as the recent one, which is expected to continue in the short term with the consensus estimate for FY2026 non-GAAP EPS at $1.28, or just $0.03 better than FY2025. These estimates are a reflection of POWI’s own outlook, which calls for modest levels of growth in FY2026, similar to FY2025. From the Q4 FY2025 earnings call:

“I think for '26, we're planning on, I'll say, similar growth levels year-over-year. As Jen said, though, it is very early in the year, and we're going to have to see how demand plays out in the first half. It has been lumpy in certain areas to date. But net-net, we're going to be planning for similar growth. However, I will say we're going to be cautious in our investments until we see those bookings really taking form and the step-ups that we expect to see, making sure that they are happening before we dive in deeper to certain investment areas. So you're going to see us be cautious on that as we are in '26. But we are optimistic over -- as Jen said, over the next couple of years, the markets that we're entering have great opportunity for us to step up that growth rate in the outer years.”

This soft growth outlook helps explain why the Q4 FY2025 report was essentially a nothingburger in terms of moving the stock when it was released in February, as shown in the chart posted earlier, which shows how the stock continued the flattish, sideways action after the report’s release that it exhibited before the report’s release. However, it needs reminding that POWI did leave open the possibility the outlook could be revised higher as FY2026 goes by.

POWI also alluded to faster growth in the coming years, even if it did not provide any numbers, and these are those who believe POWI will do better down the line. For instance, Wall Street earnings estimates for FY2027 range from $1.60 at the low end to the most optimistic estimate of $2.31 at the high end, which implies YoY increases of 25.0% and 80.5%, respectively, using $1.28 in FY2026 as the base.

The next earnings call could be significant in that investors will be looking to see if POWI will revise its initial outlook to something with stronger growth. Any clues that growth at POWI will accelerate to the point that it can begin to recoup the substantial ground it lost in terms of revenue and earnings since the highs of FY2022 could serve to give the recent rally another lease on life.

On the other hand, if the outlook remains tepid, like the last report, or worse, becomes weaker, the stock could get pummeled, especially after the recent spike, because it would essentially imply the rally in the stock price was undeserved. While current FY2027 estimates call for stronger growth, there is nothing stopping them from being revised downwards.

Keep in mind POWI never actually stated growth would be anywhere as strong. It also never explicitly said it would happen in 2027. POWI simply suggested growth could step up from the current levels of growth in the mid-single digits or so, which could just as well mean high single digits instead of the mid-single digits, but well below even the most conservative of Wall Street estimates.

How POWI could try to boost growth

As shown earlier, POWI has been rather lackluster in recent years when it comes to growing the top and the bottom line. On the other hand, the stock price has soared higher, and if POWI is to justify the much higher valuations that came with it, POWI will need to boost growth, or the stock price will not be able to stay aloft for very long.

This growth does not have to come right away, but there has to be stronger growth ahead, or one can legitimately make an argument that POWI is overvalued. For instance, according to the methodology espoused by Peter Lynch, POWI, trading at a P/E ratio of 52.8x, needs to grow non-GAAP EPS at a CAGR of 52.8% in the next 3-5 years in order to be considered fairly valued relative to growth.

POWI has not called for growth anywhere as fast. It is also much faster than the growth POWI was able to achieve in recent years. However, the market may be willing to settle for something less in the short term if POWI can show just enough to think that faster growth is likely further down the line. Many semis have been able to achieve this in recent times by fetching valuations that seem much higher relative to their growth because of the perception that growth could accelerate further, usually due to AI.

POWI could attempt something similar by attempting to duplicate the growth potential inherent to AI. POWI has not had much exposure to AI since it is traditionally more focused on the consumer side with consumer products like household appliances and smartphone chargers, but the new CEO did mention after taking office that POWI would attempt to increase its exposure to data centers by developing products for them. For example, potential customers may be interested in InnoSwitch for auxiliary power in datacenters.

This particular InnoSwitch is made using gallium nitride, or GaN, which has the potential to result in higher efficiency and thus lower cost. This could be useful for data center applications since they tend to consume a lot of electric power. POWI has really focused on incorporating GaN in its products in recent years, which is where POWI can try to make inroads in the data center market. GaN could make a difference for POWI.

Could POWI pull a rabbit out of a hat at the next earnings report/call?

Speaking of GaN, if POWI pulls a rabbit out of a hat and does something unexpected during the next earnings release/call, then GaN may be at the center of it. POWI has a fab-lite business model, which entails using third-party foundries to do the actual manufacturing. Key partners are Lapis Semiconductor, Seiko Epson Corporation, and X-FAB.

The first two are based in Japan, which is also why a large amount of the silicon wafers supplied to POWI come from Japan. These foundries in turn rely on GaN substrates that are primarily supplied by Japanese companies like Sumitomo Chemical, Shin-Etsu Chemical, Mitsubishi Chemical, and others. However, the raw gallium is not from Japan, but almost exclusively sourced from China.

The issue here is that China has imposed restrictions on the export of gallium to Japan through export controls. This has greatly reduced supplies, although existing inventories have cushioned the impact. Gallium prices have still greatly increased, and it is possible POWI may be faced with cost increases as its suppliers need to pay a lot more than before, which could mean higher prices for customers like POWI.

This issue could affect profits and sales at POWI and the outlook by extension. If POWI sees some sort of impact because of the disruptions in the gallium market, then this could sway how the upcoming report is perceived by the market, especially since any adverse impact on growth would come at a bad time, so shortly after the spike in the stock price, and the higher growth expectations inherent to higher valuations.

Investor takeaways

Up until fairly recently, POWI was in a downtrend, a major obstacle to long POWI. But POWI was able to break the trend with a huge rally, particularly in the month of April, which saw the stock go vertical at one point. However, while the gains are impressive, an argument can be made that the foundation supporting the rally does not look all that sturdy, which is reason to doubt its sustainability.

The recent bidding up of the stock price seems to be based on the assumption growth will accelerate, but this is a risky assumption to make with no clear catalyst for this acceleration of growth to be found. POWI itself has not called for growth anywhere as fast. On the contrary, the current outlook calls for modest growth in FY2026, similar to FY2025.

POWI left open the possibility growth could be faster in the coming years, but that is not the same as affirming growth levels as indicated by some of the more optimistic growth estimates by some Wall Street firms. So even if growth does speed up, it is not guaranteed to hit a level fast enough to satisfy the much higher stock price.

Growth could even remain at the current levels, or slightly better, which is incompatible with the fast growth current valuations for POWI demand, including a P/E in the low fifties. This is also using non-GAAP, which leaves out a number of items. If GAAP is used and these items are included, then valuations are much higher.

While the spike in the stock price has pushed up valuations, the issue is that growth at POWI is not anywhere close to sufficient to warrant these elevated valuations. The most recent outlook for FY2026 only calls for modest growth, in the mid-single digits or so, although POWI did leave out the possibility that growth could get better down the road. The latter is something that will need to happen a lot of to justify the large increase in valuations.

The upcoming report on May 7 could shed light on whether POWI sees an acceleration in growth or not. At this time, there is little to suggest POWI will upgrade its growth outlook, let alone increase it to the extent needed to warrant the huge increase in the stock price. On the contrary, it is possible POWI may reveal disruptions in the supply chain involving GaN, which could impact upcoming quarterly results as a result of, for example, higher input costs and the impact that could have on margins and profits by extension.

It’s also worth pointing out that POWI has a history of having to revise its initial outlook to the downside. That was under the previous CEO, so it is possible the new CEO may have a better handle on this. Still, the fact remains that POWI cannot only not afford any slowdown in growth, but it also needs much faster growth than it has delivered in recent history. This looks difficult.

I am therefore neutral on POWI with this in mind. The stock has rallied, but there is little concrete at this time to support the rally. Growth is not growing fast enough to warrant paying the high multiples the stock goes for, nor is there reason to believe fast enough growth is around the corner, something even the current outlook for FY2026 from POWI agrees with.

There seems to be a major disconnect between what POWI has or is likely to deliver in terms of growth and what is required in terms of growth to support the recent increase in valuations. The risk is that this disconnect comes to the forefront at the next earnings report/call, which may trigger a reassessment of POWI after the spike in the stock price.

While there are some earnings projections calling for much faster earnings growth in FY2027, all current signs suggest this will be difficult to achieve. The most recent earnings call actually revealed that POWI itself is not totally sure of demand. Unless POWI radically revises its outlook at the next earnings call, and as things stand, the risk for POWI is therefore to the downside after the huge appreciation in the stock price, particularly in April.

data.title: TSMC: Expect More All-Time Highs Ahead
data.description:
In late January, I upgraded Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) back to a buy rating since growth was set to reaccelerate and the valuation had corrected. I concluded that my downgrade back in November to a hold rating was premature, and as you can see in the chart below, the stock has since advanced by another 14%. In fact, it hit an all-time high just recently. The chip fab giant reported their latest round of financial results just in mid-April, and so today I have decided to take a close look and see if the bull thesis is still going strong now.

Below, it is shown that Q1 was an impressive quarter for TSMC, all things considered. Both business momentum and profitability are in favorable positions. While guidance isn't perfect and there are risks like the Iran war, the company's long-term outlook continues to be solid as they push ahead with new processes and expand their U.S. supply chain. With the valuation only slightly above where it was in my previous article and still below levels seen in late 2025, I have decided to reiterate my buy rating on TSMC.

An Inflection Point Is In

When looking at TSMC's top-line results, it is clear that there is no shortage in demand right now as the AI revolution continues to unfold. CFO Wendell Huang stated that "[their] business in the first quarter was supported by strong demand for [their] leading-edge process technologies."

In their local currency, the company saw revenues grow by 35.1% YoY to NT$1.1341 trillion, which is a steep acceleration from the previous quarter's 20.5% increase. In USD terms, they generated revenues of $35.9 billion, up 40.6% YoY. This is an equally impressive acceleration and shows that business activity is strengthening. Do note that back in Q4, there was a top-line growth slowdown both in terms of their local currency and USD, and so it seems that there has been a bit of an inflection point for the business recently.

Lastly, TSMC beat USD revenue expectations by a rather impressive sum of $404.52 million to show outperformance, and so at this point business is booming for the world's most important chip manufacturer.

Revenue Mix Could Be Stronger

As a whole, TSMC's advanced technologies (7 nm and under) made up 74% of their total wafer revenue in Q1. This is up from 73% in the year-ago quarter, which is a good sign. However, back in their Q4, advanced technologies accounted for 77% of their wafer revenue. Furthermore, their state-of-the-art 3nm process represented 25% of wafer revenues in Q1, down from 28% in the previous quarter. This may be a bit disappointing.

In the long run, TSMC's business is supported by continued adoption of the most advanced solutions, and so while the major acceleration in top-line growth discussed above is encouraging, it would have been nice to see stronger adoption of their top technologies like 3nm. At this point, I wouldn't consider this to be a major concern, but investors should definitely keep a close eye on these numbers from now onwards.

Margin Performance Is Impressive

TSMC's margins show broad strength, and so it seems that their profitability is in a very robust position right now. As you can see in the table above, gross margin expanded 7.4 percentage points YoY. Given the limited global production capacity for semiconductors right now, there is little question that the company has significant pricing power. The company isn't just relying on this, however.

For operating expenses, there was a 10.4% increase YoY, and since this lags the revenue growth rate significantly, the business is getting run in a very efficient manner. As a result, operating margin saw an even larger expansion of 9.6 percentage points YoY to 58.1%. Overall, margin performance was very impressive, and that worked its way down to the bottom line as well. As shown above, their EPS soared by 58.3%, and in USD terms, they beat earnings per ADR expectations by $0.16.

Other Key Metrics

When looking at some additional key metrics that have been derived from their balance sheet, it is increasingly obvious that the business is in a very healthy state. For A/R turnover days, the decline from 28 to 26 YoY is a signal that they are collecting efficiently, and of course that's good news for their cash flows.

For inventory turnover days, there was a slight increase from the previous quarter, but the YoY change still shows that products are not getting stuck on their shelves. Lastly, in terms of financial liquidity, you can see that their current ratio is at a very safe reading of 2.5. In fact, at this point investors may find this metric to be indicative of excessive liquidity, but nonetheless, the above table shows that TSMC is in a fine financial position.

Financial Outlook

TSMC provided both quarterly and annual guidance as a part of their earnings release. Starting with the expectations for Q2, the company sees revenues in the range of US$39.0 billion to US$40.2 billion. The midpoint was significantly above consensus and implies a growth rate of 32% YoY. This would be a notable deceleration from what we saw in Q1 but nonetheless shows that business activity will again be robust.

As for gross margin, they expect the metric to be between 65.5% and 67.5%. Since just 58.6% was reported for 2025 Q2, strong pricing power seen in Q1 is set to continue. Lastly, they are projecting an operating margin midpoint of 57.5%, which implies expansion of 7.9 percentage points. However, do note that the gross margin improvement is also expected to be 7.9 percentage points, and so in Q2 we may see limited operating efficiency increases. Nonetheless, I generally view Q2 guidance as respectable and indicative of further business strength.

Looking further out, TSMC stated that they expect to see full-year 2026 revenue growth of more than 30% in USD terms. Back in 2025, the company reported annual growth of 35.9%, and so there might be some slight deceleration. Still, a growth rate north of 30% can't really be complained about, especially given the size of their business and the capacity constraints that they are facing.

Recent Developments

Even though TSMC reported earnings just around two weeks ago, there have been some recent developments that are noteworthy here. Firstly, on April 22nd, it was reported that the company had debuted two new manufacturing technologies. Regarding these two new releases, Seeking Alpha's Tiyashi Datta wrote

One is the new A13 process, which is a direct shrink of its A14 node announced in 2025 and most likely will be useful for artificial intelligence chips, and another one called N2U - a more affordable option to make chips for mobile applications, laptops, and AI.

While production is still some way off as of now, it should be encouraging for investors to see that the company is continuing to conduct R&D with the aim of providing better solutions for chipmakers.

On the same day, it was also reported that TSMC is opting to continue using their existing ASML (ASML) EUV machines instead of upgrading to the new "high NA" EUV machines. Analysts later stated that this might boost TSMC's margins, and I agree with this view. By deferring the purchases of these $400 million next-gen lithography machines, there is little question that profitability metrics should benefit.

Just days ago, TSMC announced that they are planning to open an advanced chip packaging facility in Arizona by 2029. They obviously have fabs in the state already, but note that as of now, many of the chips that are getting produced there are still having to be shipped back to Taiwan for packaging. This reportedly adds time and cost to the supply chain, and so it makes sense for them to invest in a packaging facility in the United States. Over time, benefits should show up in their margins as well.

Risks

In their latest earnings, CFO Huang mentioned the possibility that the prices for key chemicals and gases could increase due to the conflict that is happening in the Middle East. Specifically, he stated that "there may be an impact on our profitability, but it is too early to quantify the impact." If the Iran war continues indefinitely, TSMC could see higher costs, and of course that will affect their profitability. Their financial outlook discussed earlier doesn't seem to include this geopolitically related risk, and so there is a chance that their margin could be dented. Investors should definitely be aware of this when considering the stock.

Valuation Is Still Opportunistic

Since my previous article, TSMC's forward P/E ratio has edged up from 23.29 to a reading of 25.50. At current levels, the multiple shows that the stock is at just a very mild premium to the IT sector, and so I believe that there is still a good amount of opportunity for long-term investors now.

In today's analysis, Q1 was shown to be a robust quarter. Revenue growth accelerated significantly, and both gross and operating margins expanded to show off strong profitability. Guidance for Q2 wasn't perfect, but as a whole, the near-term outlook is respectable. While there are risks like the Middle East conflict, the company remains very well positioned for the long term as they continue to develop new processes and invest in the U.S. supply chain. At this point, there is very little to suggest that AI tailwinds are fading or that the growth story is getting derailed.

With their current fundamentals being solid and the long-term outlook being arguably as strong as ever, the current valuation remains opportunistic for investors. Even after the recent expansion, the forward P/E still sits below levels seen in late 2025, and so the risk/reward seems favorable.

Final Thoughts

As mentioned in the introduction, TSMC has shown very respectable gains of around 14% since the publication of my previous update and has just recently hit all-time highs. I believe it wouldn't be unreasonable to expect continued outperformance moving forward. Keep in mind that in this day and age, TSMC is basically a company that keeps the entire world spinning. With the forward P/E at just a mild premium to the IT sector, I would say investors have the opportunity to pick up the world's arguably most important company at a very modest price tag. Therefore, I have decided to reiterate my buy rating on TSMC.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: InPlay Oil: Timing Financial Leverage Just Right
data.description:
InPlay Oil (IPOOF) closed the last acquisition mentioned in my last article in April 2025. Now the debt paydown got off to a very modest beginning in fiscal year 2025. But then came the higher commodity prices thanks to the Iranian situation. Even though this company has a hedging program designed to assure a cash flow, there is production that is exposed to those more favorable commodity prices. That is likely to enable the fast-deleveraging process favored in a very volatile and low future visibility commodity market.

The company has between 60% and 70% hedged (very roughly) of the total production in the latest corporate presentation. But that leaves a significant amount of production to be sold at market prices. That significant amount is likely to prove very material to both cash flow and net income. The reason is that both are a fraction of revenue.

The increase of the commodity prices heads straight to the bottom line (compared to the lower prices that existed) with only a few deductions. Costs, on the other hand, were basically paid for at the lower commodity prices. Those costs do not change much just because the prices rose. So that increase heads straight to the bottom line" with only a few deductions.

That means that this company timed its financial leverage just in time for higher prices to increase revenue (and help with debt repayment). Luck definitely counts in this business.

(Note: InPlay Oil Is A Canadian Company That Reports Using Canadian Dollars Unless Otherwise Noted)

Management was already increasing production because the acreage acquired was just that profitable. Now, increasing commodity prices will raise EBITDA, while debt repayments attack the debt ratio denominator. That debt ratio is going to be improving both parts of the ratio at the same time. That may well make for a record pace of improvement.

More importantly, the shareholders benefit from the financial leverage because commodity prices rose so much in a short period of time, and they are likely to stay high for a while. The whole situation could lead to a rapid balance sheet reset that only happens once in a very long time.

Luck counts in this business, and this company appears to be about to get very lucky. Adjusted free funds flow is about to go through the roof thanks to those materially higher commodity prices.

Net Debt

After the acquisition closes, there are transaction costs and assimilation (and optimization) costs that do not recur. That keeps any debt repayments to a minimum (if at all).

The debt ratio, as shown above, was not all that bad. But it is about to get a whole lot better.

The repayment schedule is generous enough that the company will likely build up a considerable cushion. It may in fact mean repaying a significant amount of debt while commodity prices are high.

Much depends upon the priorities that management has set up. But current commodity price levels likely would allow a debt repayment of C$30 million roughly per quarter combined with some growth (and the dividend).

The fact that the acquisition brought a whole lot of production that is likely to grow in this environment is "icing on the cake".

Acquisition Progress

Note that the acquisition was very profitable even before management improved the situation.

The key here is that 100,000 of anything reasonable in the first year is likely to make for an extremely profitable situation in nearly any environment. But in the current situation it may turn out to be a "license to print money".

Capital money goes a whole lot further if two wells can be drilled using the same money (due to fast paybacks) in the corporate fiscal year. That could possibly happen with this company when the wells produce as much as shown above in the first year of production.

Acquisition History

This is a management team that has built and sold companies before. So it is not unusual for a management like this to be able to handle relatively large acquisitions that are generally beyond most small company managements.

This company has actually grown production through acquisitions that materially added to production levels. The fact that this latest acquisition headed production towards the 20,000 BOED level (where there might be some cost savings available) is very understandable when the management experience can likely handle the relatively large production jump.

Risks

There is always the risk that something currently unforeseen occurs to bring down commodity prices earlier than expected. Future commodity prices are both volatile and have very low visibility. That can make the tentative higher-than-expected deleveraging rate somewhat tentative until it happens.

Assimilation of any relatively large acquisition can be somewhat daunting for any management. For this company, management experience means a lot to make a strategy like this work. Nonetheless, any acquisition can fail to meet the expectations of management.

People that build and sell companies are an especially talented group of people. Replacing senior managers in this situation if a loss of services occurs could prove to be a real challenge. Such a situation may set the company back at least temporarily and maybe longer.

Summary

InPlay Oil remains a strong buy idea. This management definitely got lucky. But things were in good shape, and management had plans to repay at least some of the debt (and likely refinance the rest).

The balance sheet was clearly in good shape before commodity prices rose. Now, the balance sheet will be in much better shape with the higher commodity prices.

That makes the dividend shown above very safe for the time being. Since most investors report a long-term average return in the 8% area, that dividend currently represents almost 6% of that 8% total return. Investors only need the stock price to appreciate another 2% to get to average. But that means that there is an excellent chance to head into an above average return or possibly even an eventual homerun.

Given the current situation, the results from the previous year do not mean much, as the company acquired quite a bit last year, and a lot of nonrecurring costs are in the results. Going forward, things look excellent for this stock.

This management made it through fiscal year 2020 (and even made an acquisition towards the end of the fiscal year). There were a lot of small companies in dire straits and worse in fiscal year 2020.

Since Canada has Spring Breakup when operations are materially reduced, there is every chance that the guidance will be revised at some point in the second half of the fiscal year to account for the effects of materially higher commodity prices.

It is very possible that net debt could be cut in half this fiscal year thanks to those commodity prices.

Since this management has built and sold companies before, I would tend to hang on to the company stock until management sells the company or until the stock price gets way ahead of itself.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: General Motors: Guidance Raise, 10% EBIT Margins, Cheap
data.description:
General Motors (GM)’s published higher-than-expected earnings and revenues for the first quarter on resilient pickup truck sales in North America as well as a favorable ruling with regard to tariffs. The Supreme Court ruling specifically allowed the automaker to increase its FY 2026 guidance for adjusted EBIT and adjusted EPS... despite an expected headwinds resulting from the war with Iran in the Middle East.

While General Motors projects to see higher cost inflation, I believe the investment setup is still very much robust, given the automaker’s strong free cash flow and continual strength in U.S.-driven ICE demand. The EV division incurred a $1.0B non-cash impairment charge in the first quarter, which was a headwind to earnings, but the general constitution of General Motors is strong. Shares of General Motors are very attractively valued -- trading at a near-18% earnings yield -- and I expect the automaker to reach new all-time highs in 2026.

Previous rating

I recommended General Motors in January on the back of strong selling momentum for Chevrolet-branded sport utility vehicles as well as robust free cash flow guidance for FY 2026. The guidance for FCF also indicated at the time that the automaker was headed for a significant capital return as well, creating upside in the share price: A Strong Buy Despite All-Time Highs. In the most recent quarter, General Motors raised its guidance for adjusted EBIT and adjusted EPS amid a favorable ruling on tariffs and robust growth in its core ICE division. I see GM as a long-term investment with potential for accelerating capital returns -- both stock buybacks and a higher dividend -- mainly because of the strong profitability of its U.S. auto business.

Profitable U.S. business with growing earnings and margins supports value proposition

General Motors beat expectations for the first quarter on both the bottom and the top line: the automaker published $3.70 per share in normalized earnings vs. $2.62 per-share expected by analysts ahead of the Q1’26 report. GM's massive EPS beat was due to an accounting reversal related to expected expenses related to import tariffs. The revenue figure was reported at $43.6B, surpassing the consensus estimate by $71M.

General Motors generated $4.3B in first-quarter adjusted EBIT -- a key performance figure for auto manufacturers -- showing 22% year-over-year growth. This growth up-lift was driven by a resilient truck and SUV market in North America, especially in the U.S., which supported General Motors’ financial results despite a 0.9% decline in revenues due to lower EV sales and wholesale volumes.

In the most recent quarter, General Motors' business in North America was the main EBIT driver as it published $3.7B in adjusted earnings before interest and taxes, showing 12% year-over-year growth. GM's U.S. and Canada business represented a massive 86% of all profits on an EBIT level in the first-quarter, and General Motors has mainly American truck and SUV buyers to thank for this strong performance in Q1'26.

Vehicles that sold especially well for General Motors in the first quarter were the Chevrolet Silverado (+8% Y/Y), the Chevrolet Traverse (+34% Y/Y), and the GMC Canyon (+21% Y/Y), all of which are high-margin vehicles. A shift towards higher-margin SUVs, rising average transaction prices to ~$52,000 per unit (+3% Y/Y), as well as lower costs, were the reasons why General Motors' EBIT margin, on an adjusted basis, grew a massive 1.3 PP compared to the year-earlier period.

Favorable tariff ruling to boost GM's full-year EBIT

General Motors guided for full-year adjusted EBIT of $13.5-15.5B, which signified a raise of $500M compared to the automaker's previous guidance. This raise is due to a recent Supreme Court ruling that said that the U.S. President does not have the authority to unilaterally impose revenue-raising tariffs. The U.S. previously imposed tariffs, including on auto parts, on foreign nations under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. Since the tariffs imposed on importers under the IEEPA have been ruled unconstitutional, General Motors also bumped its adjusted EPS guidance up by $0.50 per share to a new range of $11.50-13.50 per share.

While General Motors raised its adjusted EBIT outlook by $500M on the bottom and high end of its guidance, the company also raised its outlook for costs amid an ongoing war in the Middle East between the U.S., Israel, and Iran. The automaker expects commodity inflation, including logistics and higher memory chip costs, of $1.5–2.0B vs. $1.0–1.5B previously.

GM’s valuation

General Motors and Ford Motor (F) are the two biggest auto and truck manufacturers in North America, and both are widely profitable, mostly because of robust demand for ICE vehicles. Because both companies are profitable based off of free cash flow and earnings, we can use a P/E ratio in order to assess their relative value propositions.

Shares of General Motors are valued at an FY 2027 forward price-to-earnings ratio of 5.7X, which implies an earnings yield of ~18%. Ford Motor trades at a comparatively high forward P/E ratio of 6.7X, which calculates to an earnings yield of ~15%. Ford is trading at a higher earnings multiplier than General Motors because of the strong performance of its Ford Pro-branded commercial segment as well as its significantly higher dividend yield (Ford pays a 5% yield while GM pays less than 1%). I detailed my views on Ford here: Better Value Than You'd Think.

General Motors, in my opinion, could trade at ~8.0X next year's earnings, under the assumption that the automaker does not see a contraction in average transaction prices and maintains its strong U.S.-driven EBIT profitability in the ICE division. An 8.0X earnings multiplier, applied to a consensus FY 2027 EPS estimate of $13.97, calculates to a fair value estimate for GM of ~$112 per share.

Risks with GM

Inflation remains the biggest challenge for automakers right now as it increases materials costs on one side, and on the other, it hurts consumers whose spending on ICE vehicles may be negatively affected. The war with Iran in the Middle East has driven petroleum prices sharply upwards, to more than $100 per barrel, which has been a catalyst for a resurgence of inflation to 3.3% in March 2026. This in turn led to a significant drop in consumer confidence in April. Higher inflation is therefore the single biggest risk factor for GM's EBIT margins and earnings prospects, as is a prolonged conflict between the U.S. and Iran.

Closing thoughts

General Motors submitted a solid earnings scorecard for the first fiscal quarter on Tuesday that showed resilient performance in GM's ICE core business. The company posted strong EBIT margin gains due to a larger amount of higher-margin SUV sales as well as rising average transaction prices. The automaker also raised its adjusted EBIT forecast due to the fact that the Supreme Court overturned the President’s tariff policy, which is turning expected expenses for companies like General Motors and Ford Motor into profit. Shares of General Motors are just too cheap given the raised guidance and stronger earnings outlook, and I maintain GM as a 'Strong Buy' investment choice.

data.title: CVR Partners: The Cash Machine I Won't Miss Out Adding More To
data.description:
Since My Last Coverage

When I, in January of this year, said how CVR Partners (UAN) should be on every investor's radar, I meant it. The unit price has done very well and provided a strong amount of alpha against what the S&P 500 has done so far this year. With the last earnings report just recently coming out, it seemed like no better time than now to do another coverage on UAN.

The macro environment still looks strong, with no immediate data point showing that prices might decline in the near term. The ongoing conflict in the Middle East is pushing up prices, but countries starting to hoard fertilizers to build up reserves is having a positive impact on pricing as well. The operating model that CVR has enables it to more easily target exactly which commodity to sell depending on the market price and what feedstock input costs are as well. This versatility was part of why I initially rated it a Strong Buy, and is still a part of why I continue to maintain this rating.

Quick Notes On Taxes And The Business Model

Instead of receiving a 1099-DIV form for tax purposes, investors would receive a K-1 form instead, which typically arrives later and requires more individual work.

Most of the distributions are also considered returns of capital, which can reduce the base cost per unit, eventually reaching $0 as time goes on. Because this is tax-deferred, it instead reduces your base cost.

I’ve already talked about the tax implications and the business model in my first coverage, so to speed things up, I’ve just included a small quote here rather than reiterate the same things again. Tax implications are especially important to consider. My stance is that MLPs like UAN are investments for the long term and not something you trade in the short term.

UAN has a very interesting operating structure in that there are two facilities, but it has a choice between which type of end product it makes. For those who already follow it, they will know it focuses mostly on UAN (urea ammonium nitrate) with some sales of ammonia as well. It’s essentially a pure play on nitrogen fertilizers.

Given how the price of urea ammonia nitrate has surged over the past few weeks, I'm a bit surprised the same hasn't been seen in the unit price, considering that this is more or less the entire business model of UAN right now. Forecasts suggest that the price could reach $0.6 per kilo in June of this year and then drop down to about $0.4 per kilo. Even though the drop is pretty steep, it’s still a lot higher than where prices were back in 2024.

But what might actually keep prices higher is the conflict in the Middle East. The Strait of Hormuz is highly disrupted, which has been the main discussion for a while now and will continue to be so because of the implication it has on global trade. For fertilizers, about 15% of them originate here, which has helped push prices higher. If there are no shipments from here, I suspect prices to remain elevated for longer than what the image above forecasts. So far there have been little to no negotiations, or at least any productive ones, between the US and Iran.

Earnings Report - The Good And The Bad

With the latest earnings report just released, there hasn’t been that much movement in the stock price. Looking at the actual figures, I can’t help but think that a bigger reaction might come soon. The sales grew at a strong 26.7% YoY, and the net income grew to $49.9 million, up 84% YoY. The unit count remained the same, so there was no dilution over the past 12 months, which is a strong sign in my opinion.

Cash nearly doubled since the end of the last quarter to $128 million. Management is clearly hoarding cash right now. Even though the distributions declined to $0.37 per unit, the company still has a large sum that could be distributed in the future. You could make the argument that this extra cash on the balance sheet in reality belongs to unitholders, just that it would come later. Right now part of this seems to be reserved for “future operating needs,” and $17.8 million is for CapEx needs.

The biggest bullish numbers I read in the report were how the realized price for both ammonia and UAN has surged by 24% and 34%, respectively. This while at the same time the price of pet coke declined by 20%. The input cost went from $42.43 to $33.94 per ton. This is the crack spread I was talking about in my first coverage taking place now. It’s why the stock is a Strong Buy as there are not that many alternatives that have this business model of cheap feedstock, which then gets turned into a very valuable end product.

What also further aided the growth for the top and bottom line was the 103% ammonia utilization rate, meaning there were no outages, both facilities working perfectly. Naturally, CVR was able to generate a lot of cash this quarter, which we saw ended up on the balance sheet.

The only negative was the distributions. I know most investors would have liked to see this be higher than $0.37 per unit, but I think that dissatisfaction will quickly disappear in the remainder of this year as CVR continues to benefit from rising sales prices and lower input costs. Overall, a very strong quarter that helps support the bullish case for CVR right now.

Fertilizer Prices And Where The Market Is Heading

That fertilizer prices have surged in price is not unknown and really is the direct result of the disruption in the Middle East. But it's a bit more complicated, of course, because not all fertilizer types originate here. But the in-direct impact is still there, like gas supplies declining and impacting ammonia production in nearby countries like Algeria, for example.

The commodities that matter for UAN are urea ammonia nitrate, ammonia, and then the feedstocks like pet coke and natural gas. So even though the price of one of the feedstocks has risen, it has been largely offset because the end product sales price has surged so much in price.

Production-wise, the US is the largest producer of UAN, with 10 million tonnes produced in 2020 and rising steadily since then. Most exports go to South America and Europe. The second largest producer is Russia, with 4.5 million tonnes in annual production. The thing is that Russia mostly consumes its UAN production domestically rather than exporting it.

If this does change, I do expect that because of the sheer volumes, it would drag down the price of UAN by a lot. The latest news shows that Russia is limiting exports to help shore up domestic reserves. This is actually very positive for CVR since it means less downward pressure on prices, and crucially so in a period when prices are surging and the business is well-positioned to leverage this to boost earnings.

Secondly, China is also imposing export bans on various fertilizer types. The 2 otherwise largest producers of UAN both imposing restrictions leave a global price floor which will remain higher than it has been historically. CVR does, because of this look, and is extremely well positioned to report higher net selling prices for the foreseeable future. I don’t think these restrictions are short-term decisions; this is, I think, a consequence of the US being focused on relying less on other countries for imports, and others are following suit. This creates a more closed-off global trade, which I project will continue pushing commodities higher, not just fertilizers, but nearly all.

Having talked about the supply side, I’d like to cover the demand side as well, more so on nitrogen fertilizers, which both UAN and ammonia are. Corn is the crop that is the most nitrogen-demanding in the world. It just so happens that the US is by far the largest producer of corn in the world. Last year corn yields also hit a record yield of 186.3 bushels per acre.

With the right amount of UAN, yields can increase by 12-15%. This significant yield boost is why UAN is a staple in corn production

With UAN having such a massive impact on the crop yields, it makes it unexchangeable for farmers. There’s no reality where it wouldn't be used; the revenues would be lower, but the expense might stay the same. Using UAN is about improving yield but also ensuring your business stays profitable. This ties into how CVR has a massive domestic buyer base that is there to create a floor for UAN prices even when demand elsewhere might be lower.

Risks

The main risks for CVR, I think, are concentrated in crop rotation risks. This is fundamentally mostly weather dependent because if at the start of the season there is plenty of heavy rainfall, applying the UAN and ammonia to the corn plants becomes a lot harder. But for the farmer, they could switch out to soybeans instead, which actually don’t require any UAN, and ammonia for growth. This is directly destroying demand for the two main products that CVR makes. So far, this sort of scenario hasn’t really played out because demand for corn is still high and seasons have remained relatively stable so far.

The second risk concerns the East Dubuque plant, which, unlike Coffeyville, runs on natural gas as a feedstock instead. Prices here have remained quite volatile but are, for the moment, in a downtrend after surging in the earlier parts of this year. Still though, any rise here will directly impact the bottom line as the cost of production goes up in line with the price of gas. It’s a commodity exposure risk, the same as for the market price of UAN and ammonia. This is the nature of investing in a commodity business, though; volatility is part of the game.

The Full Picture

CVR has been one of the better investments I’ve made this year, and the thesis once again got verified as the recent Q1 report showed strong growth on both the top and bottom lines. It was an absolute cash quarter, all thanks to the crack spread between the already cheap pet coke declining and the price of ammonia and UAN rising at high rates YoY.

All in all, the bullish thesis still stands for this fantastic MLP. With this now being my second article on CVR, I am glad to reiterate my initial Strong Buy, with the recent Q1 FY2026 just having been released.

data.title: Vanadium Miners News For The Month Of April 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the Vanadium miners news.

April saw China and Europe spot vanadium pentoxide prices and ferrovanadium prices higher for the past month.

Vanadium uses

Vanadium is traditionally used to harden steel. Chinese rebar standards are requiring more vanadium. Also Vanadium Flow Batteries [VRFBs] are becoming increasingly popular especially for commercial energy storage, most notably in China. Vanadium Pentoxide [V2O5] is used in VRFBs and Ferrovanadium [FeV] is used in the steel industry.

Vanadium spot price history

China V2O5 prices were higher the last month. The Shanghai Metal Market price on April 28 was US$10,923/t (VAT excluded) (~USD 4.95/lb), up from US$10,421/t (VAT excluded) last month.

Europe & China Vanadium Pentoxide [V2O5] Flake 98% one year price chart (not updated since March 10, 2026) (source)

As of mid April 2026, Google Gemini reports the China Ferro-vanadium (80%V) FOB price at ~US$27.50-30.30/kg, higher for the past month.

Europe and China Ferrovanadium [FeV] 80% one year price chart (not updated since March 9 and 10, 2026) (source)

Vanadium demand versus supply

An April 2021 Wood Mackenzie report stated:

Global energy storage deployment surged a remarkable 62% in 2020, with 5 GW/9 GWh of new capacity added. This brought the total energy storage market to more than 27 GWh. Furthermore, we expect the global (energy storage) market to grow 27-fold by 2030.

Global VRFB forecast growth by region 2022-2031

Large scale global deployments of VRFB's are becoming more common - chart sourced as of April 2025 (source)

VRFB installations are rapidly appearing globally, notably in China (source)

Vanadium (8.5x) & Lithium (10x) demand forecast to surge the most under the IEA Net Zero Emission scenario (source)

Vanadium market news

On April 12 Energy Storage News reported:

Powering Australia’s next phase of decarbonisation: Why long-duration storage must lead. James Costello, CEO of EORA Energy, argues that long-duration vanadium redox flow battery storage is critical to Western Australia’s decarbonisation efforts, particularly for remote mining operations...Unlike conventional lithium-ion batteries, VRFB systems are designed for long-duration discharge, often exceeding 10 hours, making them ideally suited to stabilising renewable energy generation and reducing reliance on diesel over extended periods. They are also inherently safer, more durable, and capable of operating in extreme temperatures, which is critical for remote Western Australia conditions... By integrating long-duration storage behind the meter, alongside existing generation assets, operators can begin reducing diesel consumption from day one.

On April 17 Vanitec reported: "Shenyang approves battery manufacturing project including 3 GWh Vanadium Flow Battery capacity."

On April 23 Vanitec reported:

Vanadium solid-state batteries advance toward commercialisation...MK Plus has achieved a series of coordinated advancements across technology validation, manufacturing, and market development.

On April 27 Vanitec reported:

Shanghai Electric expands Vanadium Flow Battery manufacturing capacity to 2 GWh in China. New 1 GWh production line in Anhui highlights accelerating industrialisation of vanadium flow battery manufacturing... With the completion of the new facilities, Shanghai Electric’s Anhui production base will increase from its existing 1 GWh annual vanadium flow battery manufacturing capability to a total of 2 GWh, reinforcing the continued rise of domestic investment in non-lithium long-duration storage technologies.

On April 27 Vanitec reported:

ZH Energy delivers 5,000 m³ vanadium electrolyte production line as VFB supply chain industrialisation accelerates...As VFB installations scale into larger utility and grid-support applications, the ability to produce vanadium electrolyte in a repeatable, industrial, and contamination-controlled manner is becoming increasingly important across the global supply chain.

Vanadium miner news

Vanadium producers

Glencore [LSX:GLEN] [HK:805] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

Glencore is a large vanadium producer, but vanadium production represents only a small portion of their revenue.

No vanadium news for the month.

AMG Critical Materials N.V. [NA:AMG] [GR:ADG] (OTCPK:AMVMF) ("AMG")

AMG Vanadium is a leading provider of products and services for the metals, manufacturing, refinery and petrochemical industries. You can read more about AMG Vanadium here.

On April 9 AMG announced:

Successful placement of approximately 3.25 million shares through accelerated bookbuilding...The Offer Shares were sold at a price of €34.00 per share, generating proceeds in excess of €110 million.

Largo Inc. [TSX:LGO] [GR:LR81] (LGORF)(NASDAQ:LGO)

Largo Inc. is a pure-play vanadium pentoxide producer from their Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil as well as a producer of VRFBs.

On April 1 Largo Inc. announced:

Largo reports Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 financial results reflecting the impact of U.S. tariffs on Q4 2025 sales; Stronger operating momentum with further positive copper-platinum group metals flotation test results and benefit from recent U.S. tariff relief entering 2026...Q4 2025 Vanadium Pentoxide production of 2,961 tonnes represented a 67% increase over the 1,775 tonnes in Q4 2024...Revenues of $22.3 million in Q4 2025 vs. revenues of $24.3 million in Q4 2024, an 8% decrease; full year 2025 revenues of $109.9 million vs. $124.9 million in 2024, a 12% decrease...Net loss of $17.2 million for Q4 2025.

On April 14 Largo Inc. announced:

Largo reports strong production and sales in Q1 2026; Files request for authorization to produce and sell copper, platinum group metals, nickel and cobalt as by-products of vanadium operations; and announces sole Chief Operating Officer and General Counsel promotion...Q1 2026 Highlights

  • Vanadium pentoxide ("V2O5") production in Q1 2026 increased 101.7% to 2,616 tonnes vs. 1,297 tonnes in Q1 2025...

"Vanadium prices in the U.S. have increased materially year to date and, together with the reduction in U.S. tariff barriers on Brazilian products, have enhanced Largo's ability to actively supply both the high purity market, particularly the aerospace industry, as well as the U.S. ferrovanadium market...there is a lag in price realization on Largo's reported sales, so an important portion of the recent price increase should be reflected in Q2 2026 sales revenues. In addition, most of the sales of the high-purity inventories accumulated in bonded warehouses in Baltimore as a result of the 50% U.S. tariffs will also be reflected in the second quarter, as invoicing of those sales took place in April 2026 following the U.S. tariff reduction in the middle of the first quarter. As a result, higher pricing momentum in the market should support stronger revenue realization in the second quarter than would have been the case had those units been sold in the first quarter."

Energy Fuels Inc. [TSX:EFR] (UUUU)

Energy Fuels state they are "the No. 1 uranium producer in the U.S. with a market-leading portfolio", as well as being a small vanadium producer.

On April 15 Energy Fuels announced: "Ross Bhappu to take over as CEO of Energy Fuels as company enters next phase of growth."

Ferro-Alloy Resources [LON:FAR]

FAR is operating the giant Balasausqandiq vanadium project in Kyzylordinskaya Oblast of southern Kazakhstan. FAR stated in May 2025: "On a forward looking basis, process plant operations will be conducted only when suitably profitable raw materials are available."

No significant news for the month.

Western Uranium & Vanadium Corp. (OTCQX:WSTRF)

Western Uranium & Vanadium Corp. own the Sunday Mine Complex which is an advanced stage mine property consisting of five interconnected underground mines in Colorado, USA.

No news for the month.

VanadiumCorp Resource Inc. [TSXV:VRB][GR:NWN] (VRBFF)

VanadiumCorp Resource Inc. 100% owns the Lac Dore Vanadium-Iron-Titanium Project in Quebec Canada. The Company is looking to take a vertically integrated approach and is also developing leading process technologies ‘VanadiumCorp-Electrochem Processing Technology’ and "Electrochem globally patented Electrowinning" technology.

On April 16 VanadiumCorp announced:

Results of Ambient Noise Tomography survey at Lac Dore Vanadium Project...The ANT survey results suggest that the known vanadium‑bearing geological trend identified at surface may extend to depth and could be associated with a broader structural feature... Additional work, including follow-up geophysics and drilling, will be required to test the interpretations derived from the ANT survey.

Vanadium developers

Vanadium Resources Limited [ASX:VR8] [GR:TR3]

Vanadium Resources is a junior exploration company established with the purpose of exploring and developing gold zinc, lead, copper and other mineral opportunities. Vanadium Resources owns 86.49% of a globally significant vanadium project, the Steelpoortdrift [SPD] Project, in Gauteng Province, South Africa.

On April 28 Vanadium Resources announced:

US Vanadium Non Binding Offtake Term Sheet. VR8’s Steelpoortdrift Project is advancing towards becoming a long-term source of vanadium supply for U.S. defence, aerospace and industrial markets. Highlights.

  • VR8 has executed a non-binding offtake term sheet with U.S. Vanadium Holding Company LLC1 (USV) for 100% of vanadium-bearing slag production from VR8’s envisaged next-generation critical minerals smelter (V-Iron Plant).
  • USV is a majority owned portfolio company of TechMet Ltd2...
  • USV is a U.S. based integrated producer of high-purity vanadium specialty chemicals.
  • The Term Sheet includes standard offtake provisions and specifies that: a binding offtake agreement is expected to be negotiated after completing the V-Iron Plant feasibility study; and if no binding offtake is concluded within that period, VR8 grants USV a right to match to enter into an offtake agreement for 20% of the vanadium slag produced by the V-Iron Plant.

Australian Vanadium [ASX:AVL] [GR:JT71]

Australian Vanadium is an emerging vanadium producer focused on their Australian Vanadium Project in Western Australia. VSUN Energy was launched by AVL in 2016 to target the energy storage market for vanadium redox flow batteries [VRFBs].

On March 31 Australian Vanadium announced:

Development of electrolyte production technology. AVL advances its technology platform targeted at lowering the delivered cost of electrolyte...its V-NOMAD™ electrolyte production technology platform...is intended to reduce the delivered cost of vanadium electrolyte for gigawatt-hour scale vanadium flow battery (VFB) application.

Richmond Vanadium Technology [ASX:RVT] ("RVT")

RVT now owns 100% of the Richmond Vanadium Project. It has a global Mineral Resource of 1.8Bt @ 0.36% Vanadium Pentoxide (V2O5).

No news for the month.

Phenom Resources Corp. [TSXV:PHNM] (OTCQX:PHNMF) (formerly First Vanadium Corp.)

The Carlin Gold-Vanadium Property hosts one of North America’s largest richest primary vanadium deposits, located in Nevada. Its West Jerome project targets a large scale high grade copper and zinc deposit in Arizona. Carlin has a Historic Inferred Resource 28Mt at 0.525% V2O5 (2010 SRK).

On April 20 Phenom Resources announced:

Phenom reports on MK Plus progress to commercialization, the solid-state vanadium battery company...MK Plus Co., Ltd. (MK Plus), the private Japanese battery company, towards their commercialization of their revolutionary fast charging low-cost vanadium solid-state batteries. Phenom owns a 5% equity interest in MK Plus resulting from an Off-take agreement signed July 24, 2023...MK Plus’ VSB offers significant advantages over conventional energy storage systems, including exceptionally fast charging (100 times faster than lithium batteries), superior safety, cost saving (storage system costs reduced by 75%, and cooling costs reduced by 90%), 100% recyclable and an extended lifespan (150 times more charge cycles than lithium batteries).

CleanTech Vanadium Mining Corp. [TSXV:CTV] (CTVFF)

CleanTech Vanadium's flagship project is the Gibellini Vanadium Project in Nevada, USA. Gibellini holds 127 million pounds of measured and indicated V2O5, with 195 million pounds inferred. CleanTech aims to start the first North American vanadium mine. The Gibellini Vanadium Project has also shown potential for gold exploration.

No news for the month.

QEM Limited [ASX:QEM]

QEM Limited owns the Julia Creek Vanadium in North Western Queensland, Australia. Plus acquiring the Big It Tungsten Project and the Columbite Project in USA.

On April 15 QPM announced:

QEM to acquire past-producing U.S. fluorspar, tungsten & niobium Projects. $2.645m placement. Binding term sheet executed for the acquisition of Idaho, USA fluorspar, tungsten & niobium projects. Big It Tungsten Project is one of only a handful of historically verified past tungsten producers in the entire United States with documented government shipments...Big It and Columbite Projects are prospective for seven U.S. critical minerals: fluorspar, tungsten, antimony, niobium, tantalum, REE, and gold — potential to address the most acute U.S. supply vulnerabilities.

Tivan Limited [ASX:TVN] (formerly TNG Ltd [ASX:TNG](TNGZF)

Tivan 100% owns Speewah Mining Pty Ltd, the owner of the Speewah Vanadium-Titanium-Iron Project and the Speewah Fluorite Project in WA. They also own the high grade Sandover Fluorite JV Project in Northern Territory and the Molyhil Tungsten-Molybdenum Project in Northern Territory. Also the Turiscai copper-gold Project, Baucau and Ossu Projects, in Timor-Leste.

On April 22 Tivan announced: "March 2026 Tivan Limited - Quarterly Activities Report."

On April 29 Tivan announced: "Scoping Study for Molyhil Tungsten Project."

Neometals [ASX:NMT] (NMTAY)(OTCPK:RDRUY) (OTCPK:RRSSF)

Neometals 100% own the Barrambie Titanium Vanadium Iron Project in Western Australia, but are looking at divesting the Barrambie VTM Deposit, but keeping the Barrambie Gold Project. They also own 86.1% of the Vanadium Recovery Project JV.

No vanadium news for the month; however there was some gold news and some lithium/potash news.

VRFB Companies

  • Protean Energy [ASX:POW] [GR:SHE1]

Conclusion

April saw China and Europe spot vanadium pentoxide prices and ferrovanadium prices higher for the past month. Prices remain at low levels.

Highlights for the month include:

  • Powering Australia’s next phase of decarbonisation: Why long-duration (aka VRFB) storage must lead. VRFB offers discharge often exceeding 10 hours.
  • Shenyang approves battery manufacturing project including 3 GWh Vanadium Flow Battery capacity.
  • MK Plus (private) vanadium solid-state batteries advance toward commercialisation.
